post: Trial by jury didn't originate with the American colonists. It originated in 1215. The Magna Carta established that English subjects could not be condemned without judgment by their peers. For more than 5 centuries, that principle had been a cornerstone of English legal tradition, a right colonists had inherited. Which is what made Britain's admiralty court system so pointed a provocation. When Britain needed to enforce taxes and import duties in the colonies after the Seven Years' War, it routed cases through courts where a single magistrate decided guilt. No jury. No deliberation. No verdict that a hostile colonial jury might deliver. Chris Canty of George Washington University explains Grievance 18 through the Liberty Affair of 1768: John Hancock's ship brought before an admiralty court, his lawyer John Adams identifying jury trial deprivation as the core violation even after the case was dismissed. The right the colonists defended mattered enough that the Founders enshrined it specifically in the Sixth and Seventh Amendments, ensuring the admiralty court model would have no place in the republic they were building. Episode 18 of our 27-part series is live. Join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #MagnaCarta  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #USHistory

He started his career in an ambulance. As a paramedic in Nash County, NC, Charles Oliver responded to people in crisis, and kept noticing the same thing: their circumstances were often beyond their control. He started asking a different question. What career could help people before the emergency? The answer was teaching. Today, Oliver teaches "Civic Literacy" at Roanoke Valley Early College, a mixed-grade class of 9th through 12th graders in rural North Carolina. His classroom rule is simple: "We're going to attack opinions. We're not going to attack people." Last month, one of his student groups wrote a song about immigration policy, wrestling with the tension between federal law and human dignity, that ended with this verse: "Searching for justice, trying to find / a balance of law and humanity / not just rules but morality." In February, BRI and iCivics named Oliver a Civic Silver Star Award winner, earning a $2,000 grant for his school as part of the Civic Star Challenge. EdNC profiled his story this week. It's one of the best portraits of civics education working exactly as it should. Read it here:  https://lnkd.in/evGm4ciu  #EdNC  #CivicStarChallenge  #AwesomeTeachers

The Declaration of Independence declared that all men are created equal. Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Lucretia Mott read that and had a question: all men, or all people? Their answer was the Declaration of Sentiments, introduced at the Seneca Falls Convention in 1848. It borrowed the exact language of the Declaration of Independence, then amended it: all men AND women are created equal. It wasn't a rejection of the Founding ideals, but a demand that America live up to them. BRI's A250 Mini Documentary tells the story of the convention that launched a 70-year movement, rooted, from the very beginning, in the principles of the Declaration. Watch it here →  https://bit.ly/4onYo30  #SenecaFalls  #AmericanHistory  #A250

Everyone knows "no taxation without representation." The grievance was never really about the first word. Zachary Deibel, Assistant Professor at Virginia Military Institute, makes the point in Episode 17: the Continental Congress wasn't primarily upset about taxes. They were upset about representation, the fourth word of the motto that tends to get glossed over. No taxation had been enough to get people protesting. But no representation, in a government supposedly built on English liberty, was enough to launch a revolution. Discover more and join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M New episodes every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday.  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #ConsentOfTheGoverned

Ever since its drafting, the Declaration of Independence has sparked debate. First it was a dispute about separation from the Crown; later it was an argument about equality and its meaning. Our American debateusing the Declaration as a touchstone for our principleshas driven abolition, women's suffrage, and the civil rights movement. This debate continues today as we get ready to celebrate our nations 250th birthday. Divided Over the Declaration, the new book by BRIs David Bobb and Tony Williams, traces 250 years of Americans debating what all men are created equal  means, and who it includes. This story illustrates how each generation has turned to the Declaration as a standard worth fighting for. The debate over the Declarations promises is alive today. The Bill of Rights Institute exists to make sure the next generation knows how to have it, rigorously, honestly, and without flinching. Divided Over the Declaration releases today! Support BRI and claim your signed copy:  https://bit.ly/3PUcqN4

Control who a people can trade with, and you control how they live. That's what made Grievance 16 more than an economic complaint. The Navigation Acts, first passed in the 1600s, were designed to keep the colonies tightly bound to the British economy. For most of their existence, they weren't strictly enforced, colonists traded broadly, built prosperous industries in timber and tobacco, and developed commercial relationships across the Atlantic. When Britain began enforcing these laws aggressively in the years before the Revolution, and then closed the port of Boston entirely in 1774, it wasn't experienced as stricter regulation. It was experienced as the sudden withdrawal of a freedom that had defined colonial life for generations. Grace Richter of George Washington University frames the colonists' response precisely: this was a natural rights argument, not merely a commercial one. The ability to trade freely, to determine one's own economic future, was inseparable from the liberty the colonists believed they were owed. Episode 16 of our 27-part series is live. Join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #NavigationActs

Divided Over the Declaration arrived yesterday. My co-author Tony Williams and I shot a short video of that moment. Hope you enjoy it! The book is officially out tomorrow. But if you want to pre-order your copy before then, you don't have to wait. Link in the first comment.

Justice requires accountability. Accountability requires consequences. Consequences require a trial, and a trial held somewhere else is a trial designed not to convict. That's the colonists' argument in Grievance 15. The Administration of Justice Act of 1774 allowed cases involving British soldiers to be moved outside Massachusetts when a fair trial supposedly couldn't be guaranteed. John Hancock called the soldiers it protected "blood suckers." Patriots called the Act a license to act without fear of punishment. The historical irony, as Hannah Nolan of the University of Maryland explains, is that colonial courts had already demonstrated they could be fair: the Boston Massacre trials resulted in acquittals and reduced sentences, not the vengeful convictions public outrage might have produced. The Crown's stated justification for moving trials  protecting fairness  was contradicted by the evidence already on the record. When those in power can move the arena of accountability to wherever it suits them, the rule of law stops protecting everyone equally. That was the colonists' fear in 1776. It's still a live question today. Episode 15 of our 27-part series is live. Join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #RuleOfLaw  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #USHistory

Civics is supposed to be the subject that teaches Americans how to disagree well. So why are so many civics teachers afraid to teach it? Karissa Waddick with USA TODAY explored that question this week, and the answers are uncomfortable. An iCivics survey found more than half of teachers report fearing backlash for covering something the "wrong way." Basic civics concepts, the kind that have been taught in American classrooms for generations, are now described as difficult to teach. BRI President David Bobb is quoted in the piece, reflecting on what a decade of deepening political divisions has done to the classroom, and what it will take to restore civic education to what it's supposed to be: not partisan, not ideological, but grounded in the founding principles and primary sources that belong to every American student. It's a worthwhile read. Especially right now, 29 days before the nation's 250th birthday:  https://bit.ly/4uhlwBw  #CivicsEducation  #USAToday  #iCivics
